data.title: Trump finally ends Minnesota ICE op after two Americans killed
data.description:
The Trump administration says it’s ending “Operation Metro Surge” in Minnesota after two months of aggressive immigration enforcement that sparked protests and, tragically, left two U.S. citizens, Renee Good and Alex Pretti, dead.  

Border czar Tom Homan made the announcement yesterday: “I have proposed and President Trump has concurred that this surge operation conclude. A significant draw down has already been underway this week and will continue to the next week.” 

Homan says the surge is ending because cooperation between federal, state and local law enforcement has improved, meaning more immigration detentions will now happen inside jails rather than through street operations. He also says local agencies are responding more quickly and consistently to protests.  

Gov. Tim Walz acknowledged the fear won’t disappear overnight. Writing on X, “The long road to recovery starts now. The impact on our economy, our schools, and people’s lives won’t be reversed overnight. That work starts today.”  

At a press conference, Walz emphasized that Minnesota has always supported targeted enforcement and that nothing fundamentally changed in the state’s approach.  

And that’s where this gets complicated.  

For months, the Trump administration has framed this crackdown as a mission to deport the “worst of the worst.” But according to an internal Department of Homeland Security document obtained by CBS News, ICE made roughly 393,000 arrests across the country between last year and this year; less than 14 percent of those arrested had charges or convictions for violent crimes like homicide, robbery, sexual assault, kidnapping or arson.  

At the same time, ICE official Marcos Charles suggested detainers sent to “the state’s jails and prisons” weren’t being honored — but then later acknowledged the Minnesota Department of Corrections had fully cooperated. DHS has said there are about 1,360 people with ICE detainers in Minnesota prisons and jails. The Minnesota Department of Corrections says the number is closer to 300. Minnesota Department of Corrections Commissioner Paul Schnell pushed back forcefully, saying that ICE officials “continue to publicly repeat information that is inaccurate and misleading. This is no longer simple misunderstanding.”  

Since Dec. 1, the state has turned over 14 people with ICE detainers after they completed their sentences. It’s important to note, before an inmate can be turned over to ICE, they must complete their prison sentence.  

So here’s my question: If Minnesota was honoring detainers, if the dispute was about data and communication, why did it take a massive federal surge, widespread fear, and two American lives lost to reach this middle ground?  

Republicans argue sanctuary policies tie the hands of law enforcement. Democrats argue the sweeps were overly aggressive and lacked accountability. Both sides have talking points. But governance isn’t supposed to be about proving a point — it’s supposed to be about solving a problem.  

Politics is hard. Immigration is hard. But when cooperation was possible all along, and when the facts were this murky, escalation was not inevitable. It was a choice. And the cost of that choice is something Minnesota is now left to live with. 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: Graham: 'Who gives a s— who owns Greenland? I don't'
data.description:
Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.), who is closely allied with President Trump, distanced himself from the Trump administration’s maneuvering to seize control of Greenland, declaring in an interview: “Who gives as s— who owns Greenland?”

Graham downplayed the significance of the Trump administration’s aggressive posturing to take over the mineral-rich territory, which belongs to Denmark, a bid for territorial expansion that has stressed relationships with NATO allies.

“Here’s what I tell my European friends: Greenland is behind us but the goal is to get outcomes. Who gives a s— who owns Greenland? I don’t,” Graham told Politico’s Jonathan Martin during an interview on the sidelines of the Munich Security Conference.

Graham said the United States’ longstanding European allies should stop worrying about Trump seizing Denmark’s sovereign territory, arguing Trump has achieved the assurances he needs to build up a major U.S. military presence on the vast island.

“If you’re nervous, have a beer, go see a doctor. Stop being nervous,” he quipped. “The point is everybody loves NATO. I love it more because people are doing more,” referring to increased defense spending by NATO’s European members.

“How many of you believe NATO would be contributing this much money if it weren’t for Trump?” Graham asked. “He’s giving them a reason to do it and they’re doing it so let’s celebrate.”

He argued that Europe and the United States have collaborated to make Ukraine’s army “the strongest army in Europe,” asserting “it’s not even close.”

He said Trump will build out defenses in Greenland to deter Russian and Chinese expansion into the Europe.

“The point is Greenland is going to be more fortified because Donald Trump, once he feels like it’s his brand or he has some buy-in, is going to go big,” he said.  

Graham is leading a congressional delegation trip to the Munich Security Conference this weekend with Sen. Sheldon Whitehouse (D-R.I.).

Sens. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska), Mark Warner (D-Va.), Chris Coons (D-Del.), Richard Blumenthal (D-Conn.) and Steve Daines (R-Mont.) are some of the senators participating in the trip.

data.title: Watch live: Trump delivers remarks to troops in North Carolina
data.description:
President Trump will speak to troops and military families in Fort Bragg, N.C., on Friday afternoon, where he is expected to highlight the specialized forces that assisted in the U.S. military’s capture and arrest of ex-Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro.

Trump, who touted the Jan. 3 mission as a major win for U.S. national security, has signaled his administration will help run Venezuela and its vast oil reserves. Acting President Delcy Rodriguez has pushed back on that notion, claiming Maduro is still in charge even as he faces drug trafficking charges.

First lady Melania Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth are expected to attend his remarks in North Carolina.

The trip also comes as the president has ramped up his travel to key states ahead of the 2026 midterm elections to promote his agenda from economics to foreign policy.

The event is scheduled to begin at 1:30 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above

data.title: This court case could decide the future of childhood vaccines
data.description:
Every parent in America wants their children to be happy and healthy, and to enjoy all the things they love to do. Vaccines help make that possible. Children deserve to grow up without fear that exposure to a contagious disease would shatter their lives.

For decades, government health leaders have helped make that possible by letting parents know exactly which vaccines all children should routinely receive. Parents did not have to be scientists to know what to do. They could follow a clear schedule in consultation with their children’s medical providers for peace of mind.

As a parent, a pediatrician and a former health official, I have experienced firsthand how well this system works. But now, the Trump administration is trying to tear it down.  

Federal health officials recently announced that six vaccines previously recommended for all children should no longer be a part of routine care. Instead, they are now classified as “shared clinical decision-making.” That may sound reasonable, but in practice it will likely lower vaccination rates by sowing doubt among parents as to whether vaccination is right for their children.

A federal district court will soon hold a hearing on a lawsuit filed by the American Academy of Pediatrics and other medical and public health groups challenging the administration’s decision to upend the childhood vaccine schedule. As a member of the academy and head of the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation — which has joined an amicus brief supporting the plaintiffs signed by several organizations and more than 100 health and medical experts — I hope the court prevents the administration from implementing these changes.  

In my more than 30 years of practicing pediatrics and 13 years at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, I tackled a wide variety of health challenges and even national health disasters that confront children and families from all walks of life. Whether I was looking out for one child in my office or millions of children in our country, I knew that the government had my back.

In my practice, I relied on the CDC for sound vaccination advice to give my patients. At CDC, I ran the emergency response office under a Republican president and was acting director under a Democratic one. Regardless of which party controlled the White House, federal health officials always supported our work. 

But those days are gone.  That’s why this case matters so much.  

The new recommendations are not designed with children’s health in mind. They seem designed to plant confusion, create barriers to access, stoke suspicion, spread fear and push more parents to say no to vaccinating their children. If these changes are upheld, vaccine-preventable diseases will become even more common, and families across America will suffer.  

The vaccines that are no longer routinely recommended for all children provide critically important protection against some truly scary viruses. Take Rotavirus, for example, which can cause severe diarrhea and dehydration. During my training in Baltimore, I remember the devastating despair of a mother whose child died from this infection and the joy I felt when an effective vaccine finally became available.

There’s also Hepatitis B, which can cause liver failure; respiratory syncytial virus, the leading cause of infant hospitalization in America; even the flu, which has killed hundreds of mostly unvaccinated children in recent years. Routine vaccination for all of these has led to significant declines in cases, hospitalizations and deaths. Yet our health leaders are opening the door to a resurgence of childhood illness.

As our amicus brief notes: “These harms are urgent and will be irreparable.” Sadly, that warning is being ignored by our nation’s Health secretary, Robert F. Kennedy Jr., who has devoted the better part of his life to casting unfounded aspersions on vaccines. As families encounter a severe flu season for kids and widespread measles outbreaks, he is using his position and authority to make it harder for families to protect their children. It is breathtakingly irresponsible and dangerous.

A federal court is now in the process of deciding whether our nation’s vaccination system will remain standing or be dismantled. The decision will affect physicians’ ability to properly practice medicine, parents’ ability to care for their children and children’s ability to grow up healthy. The stakes could not be higher. I hope the court agrees. 

Richard Besser, MD, is president and CEO of the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation and former acting director of the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

data.title: Judge blocks planned Trump administration health cuts to Democratic states
data.description:
The Trump administration cannot proceed with plans to rescind $602 million in public health grants from four Democratic-led states, a federal judge in Illinois ruled Thursday.

U.S. District Judge Manish S. Shah determined that the four states — Colorado, Illinois, California and Minnesota — were likely to succeed in their claim that the government’s desired termination of the grants was based on “arbitrary, capricious, or unconstitutional rationales.”

Shah, an appointee of former President Obama, blocked the administration from taking any action for 14 days while litigation is ongoing.

The states’ attorneys general sued several Trump officials on Wednesday, about a week after the White House Office of Management and Budget (OMB) announced it had ordered the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) to claw back millions in funding from blue states.

Those cuts were targeted at “states fraught with waste and mismanagement,” according to an OBM spokesperson.

The states framed the administration’s directive as “part of their ongoing campaign to punish Plaintiff States using the tool of federal funding for partisan political purposes,” arguing that it would harm “essential public health infrastructure” as well as the testing and treatment of deadly diseases.

Among those initiatives on the chopping block was the Public Health Infrastructure Grant (PHIG), a multi-billion-dollar award administered by the CDC that states can flexibly use for various public health needs.

The loss of PHIG grant funding would force widespread layoffs of public health employees, reduce states’ capacity to perform public health functions and disrupt preparation for public health emergencies, among other impacts, the states argued.

“The result would be worsened patient outcomes, including greater risk of death, through the loss of effective public health programs addressing community need,” their complaint stated.

A Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) spokesperson said in a statement that the grants “are being terminated because they do not reflect agency priorities.”

The attorneys general also tied the recissions to President Trump’s policies aimed at so-called “sanctuary” jurisdictions, including an executive order he issued last April that directed the Department of Homeland Security and the Justice Department to draw up a list of cities not complying with federal immigration laws.  

Shah’s order comes less than a week after a federal judge in New York extended a preliminary injunction barring the administration from slashing funding for social services programs in a separate case brought by the same four states and New York.

data.title: DHS ends deportation protections for Yemenis in US
data.description:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) on Friday moved to end deportation protections for people from Yemen, the 13th country from which the Trump administration has struck the status.

The move leaves just four countries that the administration has not sought to end what is known as Temporary Protected Status (TPS), which bars immigration agencies from deporting nationals back to countries experiencing a natural disaster or civil unrest.

Roughly 1,400 Yemenis currently in the U.S are protected from deportation under TPS.

“After reviewing conditions in the country and consulting with appropriate U.S. government agencies, I determined that Yemen no longer meets the law’s requirements to be designated for Temporary Protected Status,” DHS Secretary Kristi Noem said in a release.

“Allowing TPS Yemen beneficiaries to remain temporarily in the United States is contrary to our national interest,” she added. “TPS was designed to be temporary, and this administration is returning TPS to its original temporary intent. We are prioritizing our national security interests and putting America first.” 

Yemeni citizens have been continually protected under TPS since 2015.

When Noem’s predecessor, former Secretary Alejandro Mayorkas, redesignated Yemen for the protection, he wrote that the country was on “the brink of economic collapse.”

“Yemen has been in a state of protracted conflict for the past decade, severely limiting civilians’ access to water, food, and medical care, pushing the country to the brink of economic collapse, and preventing Yemeni nationals living abroad from safely returning home,” he wrote in the 2024 designation. 

DHS has continually faced roadblocks in court as it has tried to lift TPS, though those rulings have not always been helpful to the recipients, who in some cases have still been forced to leave the country as litigation continues.

Last month an appeals court ruled that Noem improperly ended TPS for citizens of both Venezuela and Haiti, finding that her degrading comments about Haitians indicated she was motivated by racial animus.

A concurring opinion on the matter noted that both Noem and President Trump repeatedly made statements that “were overtly founded on racist stereotyping based on country of origin,” agreeing with a lower court that the decision to do so was “preordained” and not based on need.

data.title: HHS shaking up top personnel to push Trump, MAHA priorities ahead of midterms
data.description:
Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. on Thursday announced a reshuffling of top staffers in his department as the Trump administration looks to shore up health wins that can boost GOP success in the upcoming midterms.

Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) Deputy Administrator Chris Klomp will be Chief Counselor at HHS.

John Brooks, CMS deputy administrator and the chief policy and regulatory officer, will now be CMS senior counselor. Kyle Diamantas, deputy commissioner for human foods at the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), and Grace Graham, FDA deputy commissioner for policy, legislation, and international affairs, have been named as senior counselors for the FDA.

As CNN reported, current HHS chief of staff Matt Buckham will also move to a senior counselor role. Administration officials who spoke with the outlet said the changes came as a result of conversation between HHS and the White House.

“In just over a year, we have driven historic progress on President Trump’s health care priorities and delivered real, measurable change,” Kennedy said in a statement.

“We are restoring accountability, challenging entrenched interests, and putting the health of the American people first. I am proud to elevate battle-tested, principled leaders onto my immediate team—individuals with the courage and experience to help us move faster and go further as we work to Make America Healthy Again.”

Kennedy’s support for President Trump helped deliver a bloc of voters long critical and suspicious of the medical establishment. But many observers have noted this support is tenuous.

As Jeff Hutt, a spokesperson for the MAHA PAC, recently told The Hill, “Make America Health Again” voters aren’t necessarily those who show up strongly for the GOP during midterms.

“The Republican Party is going to have to really give those people, or express a reason to those people, why they should come out and vote, and I think that’s going to be their big challenge,” Hutt said.

In a memo to Republicans congressional leaders reported by Axios, MAHA Action forewarned that the GOP is “renting MAHA voters. They haven’t decided to purchase them yet.”

data.title: US forces complete transfer of thousands of ISIS detainees from Syria to Iraq
data.description:
The U.S. military completed the transfer of thousands of low-level Islamic State (ISIS) prisoners from detention in Syria to Iraq, completing the effort that began last month at the request of Baghdad. 

The transfer operation, which began on Jan. 21, saw the U.S. Central Command (Centcom) forces move male ISIS fighters from detention facilities in northeastern Syria to Iraqi custody.

“Job well done to the entire Joint Force team who executed this exceptionally challenging mission on the ground and in the air with great focus, professionalism, and collaboration with our regional partners,” Centcom Commander Adm. Brad Cooper said in a statement. “We appreciate Iraq’s leadership and recognition that transferring the detainees is essential to regional security.”

The U.S. military has been shifting ISIS fighters out of prisons run by Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF), backed by the U.S., as Syrian government forces have made advances in Kurdish-controlled areas of northeastern Syria, garnering control of oil fields. 

Baghdad is looking to put some of the ISIS prisoners on trial. 

“I am extremely proud of the coalition’s exceptional work,” said U.S. Army Maj. Gen. Kevin Lambert, of the Combined Joint Task Force-Operation Inherent Resolve. “The successful execution of this orderly and secure transfer operation will help prevent an ISIS resurgence in Syria.”

data.title: German Chancellor Merz says US leadership 'lost,' calls for repair of relations
data.description:
German Chancellor Friedrich Merz on Friday said that the U.S. role as a leader on the world stage is “lost,” and urged President Trump to repair the transatlantic relationship to challenge threats from China and Russia. 

“The United States’ claim to leadership has been challenged and possibly lost,” Merz told a crowd of foreign heads of state, politicians and military leaders gathered at the Munich Security Conference.

Merz’s speech echoed remarks made last month at the World Economic Forum in Davos by Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney, who described a “rupture” in the U.S.-led world order. Carney was speaking to anxiety in Europe over Trump’s threats to take over Greenland, his use of tariffs as punishment and the doubts about the U.S. commitments to NATO.

But while Carney called for a new coalition of middle powers to challenge supremacy by the U.S. and China, Merz appealed to Trump to repair ties with Europe as the best defense against an advancing Chinese military.

“In the era of great power rivalry, even the United States will not be powerful enough to go it alone,” Merz said, switching to English from German and speaking directly to Washington and Americans. 

Merz called for repairing U.S. and European relations and said a shared commitment to NATO was the best defense against threats.

“Dear friends, being part of NATO is not only Europe’s competitive advantage, it’s also the United States competitive advantage, so let’s repair… transatlantic trust together,” Merz said. 

Merz also appealed for European countries to stick together in the face of a more antagonistic United States, calling for a “new transatlantic partnership”. 

He referred back to a speech by Vice President Vance at the Munich conference in 2025 in which Vance chastised Europe as imposing on press freedoms and losing its historic culture in the face of mass migration.

“A divide has opened up between Europe and the United States and Vice President J D Vance said this very openly here at the Munich Security Conference a year ago, and he was right,” Merz said.  

“The Battle of cultures, of MAGA in the U.S., is not ours. Freedom of speech here ends where the words spoken are directed against human dignity and our basic law,” he continued.

“We do not believe in tariffs and protectionism, but in free trade. We stick to climate agreements and the World Health Organization because we’re convinced that global challenges can only be solved together,” Merz added, referencing Trump’s policies. 

“If our partnership is to have a future, we need to forge it and reason it in a new way.”

Secretary of State Marco Rubio is expected to address the conference this weekend. In a preview of his remarks, he told reporters he’d desribe “where we’d like to go, where we’d like to go with them.”

“The old world is gone – frankly, the world that I grew up in – and we live in a new era in geopolitics, and it’s going to require all of us to sort of reexamine what that looks like and what our role is going to be,” Rubio said.

data.title: UW-Madison requiring students to disclose measles vaccination status
data.description:
The University of Wisconsin-Madison announced Thursday it is requiring its students to disclose their vaccination status for certain diseases after a measles case was discovered on campus last month amid a spike nationally.

The new policy states students must tell the university their vaccination status for measles, mumps, rubella (MMR), Tetanus, Diphtheria, Pertussis (Tdap), chicken pox, Meningococcal and Hepatitis B, though they aren’t required to be vaccinated against the diseases. 

“This requirement services two main purposes. First, access to these data gives public health officials a better understanding of immunity among our campus community. Second, it helps us support individual students’ health by ensuring faster, more specific support for students who are at a higher risk when exposed to communicable diseases,” the university wrote in its announcement.  

The change comes amid a nationwide rise in measles cases, including outbreaks in South Carolina, Arizona and Utah.

“Take the vaccine, please. We have a solution for our problem. Not all illnesses are equally dangerous, and not all people are equally susceptible to those illnesses. But measles is one you should get your vaccine,” Mehmet Oz,  the administrator of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, said on CNN’s “State of the Union” last weekend.

Last year, the U.S. saw the worst outbreak of measles in decades with more than 2,000 cases recorded. 

In 2024, there were only 269 measles cases, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
